polymorphic-collections.label: Polymorphic Collections
polymorphic-collections.summary: Learn how a service can loop through mixed subclass objects and call the same method on each one through a shared base-class contract.

sketch2.title: Services should depend on the base-class contract

sc-polymorphic-collections-contract.title: Why one loop can handle mixed subclasses
sc-polymorphic-collections-contract.prompt: Why can a single loop call the same method on both `Book` and `Video` objects in one list?
sc-polymorphic-collections-contract.hint: Think about what both objects must provide for the loop to work.

help.concept: Polymorphism depends on shared behavior: different subclasses can be used the same way when they follow the same base-class contract.
help.hint_1: The loop does not need matching subclass names; it needs a shared method name.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer about both classes defining the same callable behavior.

options.a: Because Python automatically turns every object into the same class inside a list
options.b: Because both subclasses provide the same method name that the loop calls
options.c: Because lists can only store objects with identical attributes
options.d: Because the loop checks each object type before every method call

fb-polymorphic-collections-method-name.title: Complete the shared method call
fb-polymorphic-collections-method-name.prompt: Fill in the missing method name used by a polymorphic report loop.
fb-polymorphic-collections-method-name.hint: Use the method name that every object in the mixed list is expected to have.

help.concept: A polymorphic loop succeeds when every object supports the same method name from the shared contract.
help.hint_1: The loop is not choosing between different method names.
help.hint_2: Pick the method name that belongs to the shared reporting behavior, not a class name or keyword.

mc-polymorphic-collections-benefits.title: Choose the polymorphism-friendly service patterns
mc-polymorphic-collections-benefits.prompt: Which service patterns fit polymorphic collections? Choose all that apply.
mc-polymorphic-collections-benefits.hint: Look for choices where the service depends on shared behavior instead of exact subclass checks.

help.concept: Polymorphic services stay generic by calling shared methods on mixed objects, which makes the code easier to extend.
help.hint_1: A good service loop usually calls one shared method on each object.
help.hint_2: Select the options that avoid hard-coding separate branches for every subclass.

options.a: Loop through mixed objects and call `display_name()` on each one
options.b: Write one branch for `Book`, another for `Video`, and another for every future subclass
options.c: Pass a mixed list into a service that builds report lines from a shared method
options.d: Require the service to rename methods so each subclass uses a different one

dr-polymorphic-collections-loop-order.title: Order the steps in a polymorphic service
dr-polymorphic-collections-loop-order.prompt: Put these steps in the correct order for building report lines from a mixed object list.
dr-polymorphic-collections-loop-order.hint: Start with the collection, then loop, then call the shared method, then store the result.

help.concept: A polymorphic service usually follows a simple sequence: receive mixed objects, iterate, call shared behavior, and collect results.
help.hint_1: The method call happens inside the loop, not before the list exists.
help.hint_2: Think about how a `lines` list gets filled one object at a time.

tokens.t1: Receive a list of mixed item objects
tokens.t2: Loop through each item in the list
tokens.t3: Call the shared method such as `summary_line()`
tokens.t4: Append each returned string to the results list

sc-polymorphic-collections-service-focus.title: What should the service depend on?
sc-polymorphic-collections-service-focus.prompt: When writing a service for a mixed list of subclasses, what should the service depend on most?
sc-polymorphic-collections-service-focus.hint: Think about the shared promise between the subclasses.

help.concept: A polymorphic service should rely on the base-class contract or shared method names, not on exact subclass-specific branching.
help.hint_1: The service should care about behavior that every object in the list supports.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer about the shared contract rather than subclass-by-subclass checks.

options.a: The order in which the subclasses were defined
options.b: The shared base-class contract and method names
options.c: Whether every object has exactly the same attribute values
options.d: A separate loop for each subclass type

fb-polymorphic-collections-list-name.title: Complete the mixed collection example
fb-polymorphic-collections-list-name.prompt: Fill in the missing variable name for the mixed collection passed into a service.
fb-polymorphic-collections-list-name.hint: Use the collection variable that a service would loop over.

help.concept: Polymorphic services usually accept one collection of mixed objects and iterate through it using shared behavior.
help.hint_1: The blank is the list variable, not a class name or method name.
help.hint_2: Choose the plural collection name that matches a list of objects.

polymorphic-collections-sketch0.title: One loop, many object types

polymorphic-collections-sketch1.title: Shared contract, different results
polymorphic-collections-sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
A polymorphic collection works because each object promises the same method name, but each subclass can return its own version of the result.

Here is a worked example with report lines:

```python
class Item:
    def __init__(self, title):
        self.title = title

class Book(Item):
    def summary_line(self):
        return f"BOOK | {self.title}"

class Game(Item):
    def summary_line(self):
        return f"GAME | {self.title}"
```

Even though both methods are named `summary_line()`, they format the text differently.

If a service does this:

```python
items = [Book("Clean Code"), Game("Puzzle Quest")]
lines = []

for item in items:
    lines.append(item.summary_line())

print(lines)
```

The result is:

```python
['BOOK | Clean Code', 'GAME | Puzzle Quest']
```

The important idea is that the loop only knows about the shared method name. Each subclass decides what that method should return.

Line by line: read each statement from top to bottom. Notice which names are created, which values are passed into functions, and what output the code is meant to show.

polymorphic-collections-sketch2.title: Services should depend on the base-class contract
